summary: Joel highlights Google Suggest as a groundbreaking web interface innovation. He notes it uses the XmlHttpRequest technique to fetch data from the server without reloading the page, a capability that existed but was largely ignored by web developers. He argues that the latency of traditional web UIs, which require full page reloads for every interaction, makes them feel clunky compared to native applications. Google is publicly raising expectations for what web interfaces should feel like.
key_points: Google Suggest saves keystrokes but more importantly demonstrates a new paradigm for web interactivity using XmlHttpRequest
The technique of fetching server data while the user interacts with the page had been possible for a long time but was mostly ignored by developers
Full page reloads are the primary reason web UIs feel clunky compared to native GUIs
Google is setting a new public standard for web interface quality that others will need to follow
money_quotes: The latency of web UIs, in which everything you do is a slow round-trip that requires completely refetching and rebuilding the web page, is one of the reason web UIs feel so clunky compared to native GUIs.
Google is very publicly raising the bar on the quality of interfaces that people will expect from web pages.
This has been possible for a long long time, but web developers have been mostly ignoring it.
key_insight: Google Suggest's use of XmlHttpRequest to update the page without reloading publicly demonstrated that web applications could approach the responsiveness of native GUIs, setting new expectations for the entire industry.
